titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Alexander Metcalf Fisher Papers
titlestmt.author: By Ève Bourbeau-Allard

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number:3982990.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday April 17, 2018 at 19:02

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: [Papers include letters from Fisher to his parents, Caleb Fisher and Sarah Cushing Fisher, 1809-21; manuscript notes, poems, and essays by Fisher on various topics, including mathematics, science, religion, languages, and music; and clippings, eulogies, and obituaries about Fisher.]

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Alexander Metcalf Fisher Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Alexander Metcalf Fisher Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Born in 1794 in Franklin, Massachusetts, Alexander Metcalf Fisher studied at Yale College (1809-1813), and at the Andover Theological Seminary (1814). Fisher returned to Yale in 1815 to serve as a tutor, and was later promoted to Adjunct Professor (1817) and Full Professor (1819) of Mathematics and Natural Philosophy. Before his premature death in a shipwreck in 1822, he became engaged to women's education advocate Catharine Esther Beecher (1800-1878).

subject.part: College teachers -- Connecticut -- New Haven -- 19th century
College students -- Connecticut -- New Haven -- 19th century

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. comprises outgoing and incoming correspondence with family members and acquaintances, as well as third-party correspondence documenting the aftermath of Fisher's loss at sea. Of note are the fifty-sevent extent letters written by Fisher to his parents, Caleb and Sarah, over twelve years while a student and faculty member at Yale College that shed light on the early nineteenth-century academic experience. Other correspondents connected to the Fisher family include Congregational minister Nathanael Emmons (1745-1840); theologian Edward Beecher (1803-1895), brother to Fisher's fiancé; President of Yale College Jeremiah Day (1773-1867); and Yale College Professor James Luce Kingsley (1778-1852).

did.unittitle: Emmons, Nathanael to Alexander Metcalf Fisher
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to Jeremiah Day
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to Caleb and Sarah Fisher
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to Nancy Fisher
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to James Luce Kingsley
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to Princeton University

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter draft regarding a position offer at Princeton.

did.unittitle: Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to unidentified editors, drafts
Fisher, Alexander Metcalf to unidentified correspondent
Kingsley, James Luce to Caleb Fisher
"Brief heads of the plans begun accomplished during 4 days past"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Short diary recorded in September 1815, followed by a twenty-four page commentary added on 1817 Mary 24 on his mental health state ("singular fit of delirium") during the recording of the original entry in 1815.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Commentaries on visits to institutions such as Peale's Museum, the Academy of Arts, the Academy of Practical Sciences, and the Penitentiary, as well as on other activities.

did.unittitle: Annotations to the article "The Compressibility of Water" by Jacob Perkins

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the American Journal of Science and Arts 3, no.2 (May 1821).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the American Journal of Science and Arts 1 (1819).

did.unittitle: "On maxima minima of functions of two variable quantities"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the American Journal of Science and Arts 5, no. 1 (1822).

did.unittitle: "On some recent improvements in the construction of the printing press"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the American Journal of Science and Arts 3, no.2 (1821).

did.unittitle: "Remarks on Dr. Enfield's Institutes of Natural Philosophy"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the American Journal of Science and Arts 3, no.1 (1821).
